hook: 1834 glass-roofed arcade financed by a Mexican refugee heiress
facts: Built 1834 by architect Gabriel-Joseph Durand, inaugurated 1 April
Largely funded by Ramona de la Torre, a wealthy Mexican emigree
Cuts diagonally through a block, linking four streets
Listed historic monument since 1975; glass canopy restored 2015-16
w: Mexican merchants fleeing their country's wars washed up rich in 1830s Bordeaux, and one heiress, Ramona de la Torre, paid for this diagonal glass-roofed arcade to rival the Paris galleries. Opened on April Fools' Day 1834, it still slices a shortcut through four streets. Faded commerce, gleaming canopy.

hook: Bordeaux's oldest cemetery, where Goya lay headless until 1888
facts: Created 1791 on the grounds of a former Carthusian convent
Goya buried here in 1828 beside compatriot Martin Goicoechea
1888 exhumation found his skull missing; remains sent to Madrid
A Goya cenotaph still marks the spot in the cemetery
legend: They say a former model, the Marquis de San Adrian, stole Goya's head to study his brain
w: Goya died in exile here in 1828 and went into the city's oldest graveyard, laid out on a dissolved Carthusian convent. When Spain came to repatriate him in 1888, diggers found the skeleton minus its skull. Legend says a former model stole the head to read the genius in it. A cenotaph marks the empty plot.

hook: One of Europe's largest synagogues, roofed by Eiffel's workshops
facts: Built 1877-1882, financed by the Pereire, Rothschild and Osiris families
Among the largest synagogues in Europe; 36m long, 26m wide
Riveted metal roof structure made by Gustave Eiffel's workshops
Listed historic monument since 1998
w: Set back behind rue Sainte-Catherine, this vast hall counts among Europe's largest synagogues, raised 1877-82 on money from the Pereire and Rothschild dynasties. The Gothic-meets-Oriental shell hides a riveted iron roof built in Gustave Eiffel's workshops. The Nazis used it as a prison; the community rebuilt and prays here still.

n: Musee du Vin et du Negoce
hook: Wine-trade museum down vaulted cellars of a 1720 merchant's house
facts: Housed at 41 rue Borie in a building dated 1720
Once home to Irish wine merchant Francis Burke
Opened June 2008 across three semi-buried vaulted cellars
33m of barrel-ageing cellars now display trade artefacts
w: The Chartrons grew rich shipping claret, much of it through Irish and English traders, and this museum burrows into the 1720 house of one of them, Francis Burke. Down 33 metres of vaulted cellars that once held ageing barrels sit old bottles, tools and trade ledgers. The wine business, told from underground.

hook: Twin 58m neo-Gothic spires raised over the wine merchants' quarter
facts: Built 1874-1880 by architect Pierre-Charles Brun
Neo-Gothic, inspired by Viollet-le-Duc's theories
Two spires rise 58m; rose window over the facade
Holds an 1881 Wenner symphonic organ; listed historic monument
w: The merchant Chartrons wanted a church to match its money, so in the 1870s Pierre-Charles Brun gave it twin 58-metre spires drawn straight from Viollet-le-Duc's neo-Gothic playbook. Inside, an 18th-century Carmelite altar and an 1881 symphonic organ. Wine wealth, turned to stone and stained glass.

hook: Last cast-iron Baltard market hall, built against the old ramparts
facts: Built 1884-1886 by municipal architect Charles Durand
Baltard-style hall on 34 cast-iron columns
Closed 1975 with only 22 traders left, down from 124 in 1939
Restored and reopened 2015 as a cultural venue
w: Built into the line of Bordeaux's vanished ramparts in the 1880s, this iron-and-glass hall on 34 cast-iron columns sold poultry, game and fish until the traders dwindled to 22 and the doors shut in 1975. One of the city's last Baltard-style markets, it reopened in 2015 as a neighbourhood culture lab. Old bones, new use.

hook: Neoclassical royal gate on the site of a Roman Gate of Jupiter
facts: Built 1748-1753 under architect Andre Portier for Intendant Tourny
Stands where the Roman Porta Jovis, the Gate of Jupiter, once stood
Central arch flanked by two pedestrian arches and a pediment
Kings of France entered the city through it, hence 'royal gate'
w: Roman Bordeaux pierced its wall here with the Porta Jovis, the Gate of Jupiter. The 18th-century planners under Intendant Tourny swept that away for this neoclassical arch, finished in 1753, through which French kings made their entrance. Now it frames a shopping street, the empire beneath it long forgotten.

hook: Neoclassical Protestant temple grown from a wine warehouse chapel
facts: Completed 1835 to a design rooted in an earlier project by Louis Combes
Prayer first held nearby in 1776 inside an old wine chai
Single 35m nave under a barrel vault with a hemicycle apse
Decommissioned in the 1970s; listed historic monument 1975
w: Bordeaux's Protestants first gathered in a wine warehouse here in 1776, only daring a proper temple after the Revolution settled. Finished in 1835, its plain neoclassical box holds a 35-metre barrel-vaulted nave. Sold to the city in the 1970s, it spent years storing museum scenery. Reformed austerity, mothballed.

hook: 1920s maritime exchange built as a deliberate copy of Place de la Bourse
facts: Built 1921-1925 for the Chamber of Commerce and Maritime Federation
Architects Lacombe, Augereau and Louis Garros
Pastiche of the central pavilion of Place de la Bourse
Sculptor Gaston Leroux reproduced a Francin pediment and mascarons
w: When the port's shipping men wanted their own exchange in the 1920s, they skipped invention and ordered a near-copy of Gabriel's 18th-century Place de la Bourse pavilion next door, mascarons and all. Sculptor Gaston Leroux duly reran the old motifs. A maritime trading hall masquerading as the city's grandest square.

hook: Europe's tallest lift bridge, its deck rising for ships on the Garonne
facts: Built 2009-2013, inaugurated 16 March 2013
Central span lifts on four 77m towers to let tall ships pass
Longest vertical-lift bridge in Europe when opened
Links Bacalan on the left bank to La Bastide on the right
w: When a cruise ship or tall vessel comes up the Garonne, this whole bridge deck rises between four 77-metre towers to let it through. Opened in 2013 as Europe's tallest lift bridge, it stitches the old maritime Bacalan to La Bastide opposite. Watch the lift if you can time it; the river still runs the city.
